nativeRange.habitat: Tropical and subtropical Asian wetlands, marshes, and shallow stream/river margins; commonly emersed along seasonal waterways

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich substrate or supplementary root tabs
background placement with vertical room to grow
medium to high lighting for compact form and bluish tint

imageCandidates.notes: Submersed studio shot of a cultivated stem; clearly shows the light-green, narrow lanceolate (willow-like) foliage that defines the species. Best single image for ID purposes.
Emersed-form photograph from the Flowgrow plant database; useful as a secondary reference showing the willow-like leaf shape above water.

summary: Willow-leaf Hygrophila is a fast-growing Asian background stem plant (Hygrophila salicifolia) with narrow, willow-like leaves that tend to develop a distinctive blue-green tint under brighter light — the trait behind its trade name 'Hygro Blue'. Generally considered beginner-friendly, it tolerates a wide range of freshwater conditions.
careNotes:
Willow-leaf Hygrophila (Hygrophila salicifolia, formerly also listed as Ruellia salicifolia) is a stem plant native to tropical and subtropical wetlands across South, Southeast, and East Asia. It belongs to the family Acanthaceae and is widely sold under trade names including 'Hygro Blue', 'Blue Hygro', 'Willow Hygro', and 'Willow-leaf Hygrophila'. In the trade it is regularly confused with Hygrophila angustifolia, which shares the same narrow, willow-like leaf shape and similar care requirements. Submersed stems typically reach 30–50 cm, with individual leaves around 11–12 cm long and roughly 2 cm wide. Plants grown emersed (above the waterline) can exceed 1 m in height and produce small purple flowers.

The species is generally considered beginner-friendly and will grow under low to medium light without added CO2. Providing medium to high light, CO2 supplementation in the range of 10–40 mg/L, and regular macro and iron/micronutrient dosing rewards the grower with denser stems, faster growth, and the distinctive blue-green to reddish-tipped leaf coloration behind the 'Hygro Blue' trade name. Under strong light some growers also report a noticeably angular, almost triangular leaf profile.

Best positioned at the back of the aquarium as a background or upper-midground plant, where it has room to grow upward. Stems that are left untrimmed tend to become leggy over time and can shade out lower leaves, so regular trimming is recommended to encourage bushy lateral growth. The plant draws nutrients from both the water column and the substrate; a nutrient-rich substrate or root tabs are beneficial but not strictly required.
breedingNotes: Willow-leaf Hygrophila is propagated almost exclusively by stem cuttings. To take a cutting, snip the top 5–10 cm of a healthy stem, remove the lowest pair of leaves to expose a bare node, and press the stem into the substrate. The original stem will branch laterally from the node just below the cut, producing a bushier plant over time. Trimming frequently encourages this lateral branching and keeps the group full rather than tall — the preferred approach when planting as a background. Emersed plants (those grown above the waterline) produce small purple to lavender flowers and can set seed, but propagation from seed is rarely practiced in the aquarium hobby, where cuttings are far more reliable and convenient.

primary.name: Aquasabi
primary.notes: Used as the primary source: provided full binomial with author (Vahl) Nees, synonym Ruellia salicifolia Vahl, family Acanthaceae, tropical Asian origin, temperature 18-30 C (optimal 20-28 C), pH 5-8, GH 0-30 dGH, KH 2-18 dKH, medium light, optional CO2, recommended height 30-40 cm, leaf dimensions ~11.5 x 1.9 cm, cuttings propagation, easy care level, emersed flowering, and reddish new growth. Selected over Modern Aquarium and Buce Plant because it carries the most complete taxonomic and parameter data.

additional.name: Flowgrow
additional.notes: Cross-checked synonym (Ruellia salicifolia Vahl), family (Acanthaceae), distribution (South/Southeast/East Asia), temperature 18-30 C (optimum 24-28 C), pH 5-8, KH 2-18 dKH, medium-to-high lighting, CO2 10-40 mg/L, NO3/PO4/K/Fe ranges, background/midground/Dutch-street placement, cutting propagation, easy care, and willow-like leaf morphology with emersed growth confirmed.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Used for trade name 'Hygro Blue' and the description of the bluish-green tint and angular/triangular leaf appearance under good light. Also confirmed temperature 70-82 F (~21-28 C), moderate lighting baseline (looks best moderate-to-high), CO2 not required but beneficial, fast growth rate, background placement, propagation by trimming-and-replanting, and care level 'easy'.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Hygrophila (plant)
additional.notes: Used to confirm genus taxonomy: family Acanthaceae, order Lamiales, with the genus distributed across the tropical and subtropical world. (Note: H. salicifolia itself is not listed on Wikipedia's accepted-species table, but it is widely treated as accepted in the horticultural literature and by Aquasabi/Flowgrow with author citation '(Vahl) Nees', and is documented as distinct from H. angustifolia despite frequent trade-name overlap.)
